coredata.dc:title: Cellulosic schiff base hydrogel biosensor for bacterial detection with pH/thermo-responsitivity: DFT calculations and molecular docking
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tohamy, Hebat-Allah S.
coredata.dc:description: A facile green hydrothermal method was developed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using sugarcane bagasse (SB) as a raw material. The incorporation of CDs within the hydroxyethyl cellulose-acrylamide hydrogel (HEC-AM) in the presence of glutardehyde (GA) as a crosslinking agent showed bright red emission under fluorescence microscope. The hydroxyethyl cellulose-N–CDs-acrylamide hydrogel (HEC-AM@N–CDs) were used as alternative biocompatible fluorescent probes for imaging and suppression of Gram-negative bacteria such as Escherichia coli (with inhibition zones 13 mm for HEC-AM to 17 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) & Helicobacter pylori (with inhibition zones 16 mm for HEC-AM to 13 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) and Gram-positive bacteria such as Micrococcus luteus (with inhibition zones 15 mm for HEC-AM to 16 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) & Staphylococcus aureus (with inhibition zones 13 mm for HEC-AM to 14 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs). The N–CDs promote bacterial binding and internalization, leading to differential fluorescence emission depending on the bacteria's cell wall composition. DFT calculations support strong interactions between the bacteria and HEC-AM@N–CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensor fluorescence cellulosic hydrogel
Bacterial detection
Antibacterial activity
pH/thermo-responsive hydrogel
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Schiff base reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots
Hydroxyethyl cellulose-N–CDs-acrylamide hydrogel as probes for imaging bacteria

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the National Research Centre and the PRIMA International Project, between the Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT) and PRIMA, through the Joint Agreement project \u201CSustainable Antimicrobial Packaging based on a Healthy Intelligent Renewable Approach\u201D with acronym \u201CSAPHIRA\u201D.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reaction mechanism for synthesizing hydroxyethyl cellulose-N-CDs-acrylamide hydrogel.
FTIR spectra of N–CDs, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs.
The gap energies (HOMO–LUMO) (eV) were calculated for the hydrogels using DFT B3LYP/6–31G (d), as was the molecular orbital interaction between HEC, N–CDs, AM, GA, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs.
The biological activity of N–CDs as a ligand against (a) Escherichia coli PDB (7AB4), (b) Helicobacter pylori PDB (5Y2D), (c) Micrococcus luteus PDB (4EWP) and (d) Staphylococcus aureus PDB (7BGD).
Antimicrobial activity of the HEC-AM (denoted as 11) and HEC-AM@N–CDs (denoted as 12).

Fluorescence microscope for (a) HEC-AM@N–CDs before bacteria, (b) HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Escherichia coli, (c) HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Helicobacter pylori, (d) HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Micrococcus luteus and (e) HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Staphylococcus aureus.

(a) swelling behavior, (b) Pseudo-first-order, (c) Pseudo-second-order, (d) Peppas and Peppas, (e) Fick's mechanism, (f) Peppas and Franson method, (g) PH behavior and (h) Thermal behavior of HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels.

head.ce:title: Cellulosic schiff base hydrogel biosensor for bacterial detection with pH/thermo-responsitivity: DFT calculations and molecular docking

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki, Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile green hydrothermal method was developed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using sugarcane bagasse (SB) as a raw material. The incorporation of CDs within the hydroxyethyl cellulose-acrylamide hydrogel (HEC-AM) in the presence of glutardehyde (GA) as a crosslinking agent showed bright red emission under fluorescence microscope. The hydroxyethyl cellulose-N–CDs-acrylamide hydrogel (HEC-AM@N–CDs) were used as alternative biocompatible fluorescent probes for imaging and suppression of Gram-negative bacteria such as Escherichia coli (with inhibition zones 13 mm for HEC-AM to 17 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) & Helicobacter pylori (with inhibition zones 16 mm for HEC-AM to 13 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) and Gram-positive bacteria such as Micrococcus luteus (with inhibition zones 15 mm for HEC-AM to 16 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs) & Staphylococcus aureus (with inhibition zones 13 mm for HEC-AM to 14 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs). The N–CDs promote bacterial binding and internalization, leading to differential fluorescence emission depending on the bacteria's cell wall composition. DFT calculations support strong interactions between the bacteria and HEC-AM@N–CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a highly promising class of fluorescent nanomaterial due to several advantageous properties. These include low cost, exceptional stability, minimal toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, ease of surface modification, and the ability to tune their fluorescent emissions [1–3]. CDs light up across a wide range of colors when analyzed with a technique called photoluminescence because of defects such as nitrogen which absorb at 569 nm and emit at 700 nm [4,5]. Researchers are excited about using CDs for chemical sensing and capturing pollutants because they are tiny, luminescent, hydrophilic, can be tailored to target specific things, are cost-effective to produce, and are easy to manufacture [3,6]. Scientists have utilized a range of techniques to create CDs. As a result, these efforts have sparked a wave of innovative ideas and procedures, solidifying CDs as a highly promising material with vast potential for future exploration across various fields [5,7]. Red light emission from the CDs is correlated with their higher nitrogen content [8,9]. Red light from these N–CDs can reach deep into tissues without being confused by the body's natural glowing [10–13]. For this reason, instead of relying on traditional reducing agents, we'll utilize a greener solution: N–CDs made from recycled sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste (SB) with red emissive properties. The good news? We can synthesize them using an environmentally friendly microwave technique, giving new life to agricultural leftovers [14,15].

Throughout history, bacterial infections have been a significant threat to human health [16,17]. Precisely identifying bacteria is crucial for diagnosing infections and developing effective antibiotics. Excitingly, minute fluorescent CDs can alter their color and brightness depending on the type of bacteria they interact with. This makes them a powerful tool for discriminating between different bacteria, even in mixtures. Researchers have already successfully used CDs to image various bacteria, including E. coli, S. aureus, yeast, and Bacillus subtilis [18–20]. Tiny fluorescent CDs can helpdiscriminatedifferent types of bacteria. The cool thing is, the hue of their color change depending on the bacteria they're attached to [21]. In a study by Paul et al., a hydrothermal method was used to create gelatin-CDs. These gelatin-CDswere remarkable because they could bind to various types of cells, including E. coli, S. aureus, Candida species (C. albicans, C. krusei, C. parapsilosis, and C. tropicalis). When excited by light, the combination of these CDs and cells produced different colors (green, red, and blue) depending on the wavelength of the light used [22]. A study showed that the level of fluorescence reduction (quenching) depended on how well each type of bacteria stuck to CDs modified with specific receptors. This finding was utilized by Zheng et al., to create a fluorescent sensor that could detect harmful bacteria by switching its signal on or off [23]. Little is known about how microorganisms interact with CDs. Investigating how CDs affect their growth is uncommon.

Hydrogels, with their high water content, responsiveness, and safety, are in high demand for medical, biological, and pharmaceutical applications [14,24]. pH-sensitive hydrogels offer enhanced controllability [25]. Acrylamide (AM), known for its biocompatibility and pH-sensitivity, is versatile in making many products. Glutaraldehyde (GA) facilitates the creation hydrogels by crosslinking their molecules together [25]. At the same time hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) based hydrogels hydrogels are good at detecting things, making them ideal for a world that wants sustainable and effective materials for these purposes [3,14]. The nitrogen from the amine group (either in AM or on the N–CDs) nucleophilically the electron-deficient carbon of the aldehyde group in GA. This results in the formation of a new carbon‑nitrogen double bond (C=N), which is the imine bond by a reaction called Schiff base formation.

This article presents a green and sustainable method for synthesizing antimicrobial/sensing N–CDs embedded HEC-AM hydrogel nanocomposites. The nanocomposites were prepared using a Schiff base reaction, and their properties were characterized using FTIR, SEM, and antimicrobial activity assays. Additionally, DFT and docking calculations were performed to investigate their interactions. The pH and thermo-responsivity of both HEC-AM (without N–CDs) and HEC-AM@N–CDs (with N–CDs) hydrogels were also evaluated.

Bagasse (SB) was provided by Quena Company for Paper Industry Egypt. Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC 92 %) was purchased from Merck-Schuchardt, acrylamide (AM) was supplied from SD-Fine Chem Ltd. All chemicals, reagents, and substrates used in this study were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 0.03 g SB was dissolved with 0.07 g NaOH and 2.4 g urea. This mixture was left to sit overnight in a freezer. After thawing, ultrasonicator was used to break up the mixture for a short time. Finally, the mixture was put in microwave for several minutes to turn it into a yellow solid, which are the N-CDs [3,6].

To prepare Gel solution (4 %), a quantity of HEC was dispersed in the phosphate buffer (PBS) and agitated at 70 °C until it was entirely solved. The Gel solution (4 %) was mixed in a 1:1 volume ratio with AM (4 %) solution in the presence of glutardehyde (1 %) as a crosslinking agent with continuous stirring for 15 min. Then the mxture was kept at 70 °C until the hydrogel formation without stirring. The control sample (HEC-AM) is this hydrogel sample. This process was repeated with the addition of N–CDs (4 %) based on the total weight of the polymer composition. This hydrogel is denoted as HEC-AM@N–CDs. To better characterize these hydrogels, freeze-drying was used to dry them.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM)/energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) images were taken using a Quanta/250-FEG scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer. The acceleration voltage was 30 kV.
Fluorescence microscopy was performed using a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer (made in Japan) with a 150-watt xenon arc lamp.

FTIR spectra were taken with a Mattson 5000 spectrometer (Unicam, United Kingdom) using the potassium bromide (KBr) disk method. The crystallinity index (LOI) was calculated using the eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
LOI =
A 1425
A 900
(2)
MHBS =
A OH
A CH
where A1425 and A900 refer to the FTIR absorbance at 1425 and 900 cm−1, respectively. In addition, AOH and ACH refer to the FTIR absorbance of the OH and CH peaks, respectively [26,27].

The swelling behavior of the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels was observed by submerging 0.1 g of freeze-dried hydrogel samples in distilled water or buffer solution and incubating them at room temperature for 24 h. After that, the swollen hydrogels, squeezed out any extra water, and weighed them again. A special formula was used to calculate the swelling equilibrium (sweq. %):
(3)
sw eq
. % =
m t
−
m 0
m 0
100 %
where mt and m0 are the mass of the swollen gel at time t, and the mass of dry hydrogel, respectively [3,14].

Considering the first and second order kinetics:
(4)
ln
W e
W
e 1
−
W t
=
K 1
t
(5)
t
W t
=
1
K 2 We 2
+
1 We
t
We1 and We2 stand for the quantities of water absorbed by the gel at equilibrium for pseudo first and second-order models, respectively [14,26].

Peppas and Peppas described simple water-transport phenomena in hydrogel networks using the following equation (below 0.6, fractional swelling values):
(6)
ln
Wt We
= K
t n

A plot of ln
Wt We
versus ln t was used to calculate n and k, from the slope and intercept, respectively [14,28].

Based on Fick's law, a mutual diffusion coefficient (D) could be calculated:
(7)
ln
Wt We
=
4
π 0.5
Dt
L 2
n

The Peppas and Franson method was followed to estimate the water penetration velocity (υ) of the hydrogels:
(8)
υ =
dWt dWe
1 ρ
1
2 A
where Wt and We stand for the quantities of water absorbed by the gel in time ‘t’ and at equilibrium, respectively; k is the swelling constant, and is characteristic of the system under consideration and n represents the diffusion exponent that throws light on the mode of water transport into the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs gel [14].

The Gaussian 09 W program was used to perform density functional theory (DFT) calculations with the hybrid functional B3LYP (Becke's three-parameter hybrid functional utilizing the BLYP correlation functional) with the 6-31G(d) basis set exhausted by the Berny technique. Different parameters were investigated via DFT calculations, including some of the optimized geometries and ground state energies, including total energy (ET), the energy of the highest occupied MO EHOMO, the energy of the lowest unoccupied MO ELUMO, the energy gap (Eg), the dipole moment (μ), the absolute hardness (η), the absolute softness (σ), the chemical softness (S), and the additional electronic charge (ΔNmax) [29,30].
(9)
E gap
=
E LUMO
−
E HOMO
(10)
η =
E LUMO
+
E HOMO
2
(11)
σ =
1
η
(12)
S =
1
2 η
(13)
Δ
N max
=
− Pi
η

A hydrogel was made by grafting AM and N–CDs onto HEC in the presence of GA as a crosslinking agent. Fig. 1 outlines the proposed mechanism for the grafting and chemical crosslinking reactions. There are numerous OH and NH2 groups available on the HEC, AM and N–CDs that may react with GA by Schiff base reaction to form imine type bonds [31,32]. As a consequence, the crosslinked materials obtained have a complex The OH groups of HEC can form semiacetals or acetals. Semiacetals are generally less stable than acetals [31].

The surface chemistry of the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels is generally investigated by FTIR. The N–CDs, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs showed absorption bands between 3334 and 3428 (NH), 3334–3348 (OH), 1666–1675 (Amide I), 1542–1596 (Amide II), 1413–1457 (O=C–O), 1107–1151 (C–O–C) and 1039–1045 cm−1 (CN) [6,33].

MHBS (i.e. 0.56, 0.84 and 0.85) and LOI (i.e. 0.52, 1.30 and 1.31) for N–CDs, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs, respectively, which means high H-bonding strength between HEC-AM@N–CDs compared to N–CDs and HEC-AM. For HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels, the OH group was shifted from 3334 cm−1 for HEC-AM to 3348 cm−1 for HEC-AM@N–CDs (Fig. 2 ). This shift proved the stronger intermolecular H-bonding between OH groups in the grafting reaction with AM and NH2 groups of N–CDs [34,35]. The FTIR spectrum of the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels differed from N–CDs as evidenced by the increased relative absorbance (RA) of the characteristic absorbance peaks of OH (i.e. 0.51, 0.84 and 0.85) and CO (i.e. 0.59, 0.84 and 0.98) for N–CDs, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs, respectively. The oxygenated groups are high in HEC-AM@N–CDs compared to the HEC-AM due to the presence of N–CDs.
DFT calculations were employed to study the stability of the HEC, N–CDs, AM, GA, HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs. From Fig. 3 and Table S1, the results show the following:

(a) The μ for HEC-AM@N–CDs (i.e. 4.598 Debye) is higher than HEC-AM (i.e. 4.116 Debye). This may be because of the presence of more O and N atoms from N–CDs which increase electronegativity in HEC-AM@N–CDs compared to HEC-AM [36].
(b) The high negative charge in ΔNmax and increased the μ value for HEC-AM@N–CDs represent its polarity.

(c) The calculated Eg for HEC-AM@N–CDs is the lowest (i.e. 0.0089 eV) compared to HEC-AM (i.e. 0.0532 eV) which in turn prove the strong chemical reaction between HEC, AM, GA and CDs in the HEC-AM@N–CDs compared to HEC-AM due to the presence of N–CDs [37]. In addition, the Eg was the lowest for N–CDs (i.e. 0.177 eV) compared to other reactants i.e. HEC (0.2509 eV), AM (0.4508 eV) and GA (0.3548); which means the high reactivity of N–CDs which can be excited easily with small energy.
The result of HEC-AM@N–CDs (i.e. 0.0089 eV) is smaller than that of N–CDs (i.e. 0.177 eV), confirming its reactivity in comparison with the N–CDs [38].

(d) The ET of HEC-AM@N–CDs (−2085.09 au) is lower than HEC-AM (−1292.2 au) which means that the formation of the HEC-AM@N–CDs releases energy. This released energy makes it harder to break the bonds in the HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogel.
(e) The HEC-AM@N–CDs is much softer than the HEC-AM hydrogel is a good approximation of the strong energy changes between the donor (HOMO) and acceptor (LUMO) in HEC-AM@N–CDs [39].

The findings revealed that HEC-AM (denoted as 11) and HEC-AM@N–CDs (denoted as 12) exhibited antibacterial activity against both Gam-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Against Gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli displayed inhibition zones of 13 mm for HEC-AM and 17 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs. Helicobacter pylori showed inhibition zones of 16 mm for HEC-AM and 13 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs. For Gram-positive bacteria, Micrococcus luteus had inhibition zones of 15 mm for HEC-AM and 16 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs. Staphylococcus aureus exhibited inhibition zones of 13 mm for HEC-AM and 14 mm for HEC-AM@N–CDs. The promising anti-Gram-positive bacterial activity is likely due to the ability of O and N groups in AM and N–CDs to interact with lipids, proteins, and DNA/RNA of bacterial cell via H-bonds, π-π, and electrostatic bonds. Additionally, the CDs may regulate the generation of reactive oxygen species which could be inherited from surface proteins of bacterial origin [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydroxyethyl cellulose-N–CDs-acrylamide hydrogel as probes for imaging bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria are a serious threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, the development of sensitive methods for bacterial detection is crucial. Our previously synthesized N–CDs exhibit the necessary characteristics to serve as a fluorescent probe for this purpose [42,43]. Fig. S1 showed the optical properties of N–CDs. UV-spectra showed an absorption peak at 306 nm due to the π–π* transition of CC and –C–C– bonds present in the sp2 carbon electrons in the core structure of N–CDs. Additionally, a second small peak at 340 nm was assigned the n– π* transition of C = O bonds [3,43].

To explore the potential of HEC-AM@N–CDs for bacterial detection, we examined their fluorescence before and after interaction with various bacteria. Using a fluorescence microscope, we observed intense red fluorescence from HEC-AM@N–CDs before bacterial exposure (Fig. 6a). Bacterial cells were collected during the exponential growth phase and used for confocal microscopic studies. The cells- HEC-AM@N–CDs-conjugates were washed with double distilled water and their images were measured by using a fluorescence microscope. Interestingly, HEC-AM@N–CDs exhibited different imaging patterns between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The HEC-AM@N–CDs emitted bright-green light in the presence of HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Escherichia coli and HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Helicobacter pylori, while they emitted bright-red light in the presence of HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Micrococcus luteus and HEC-AM@N–CDs/ Staphylococcus aureus (Fig. 6). Despite both types of bacteria being cultured under neutral conditions, their cell membranes and cytoderm compositions differ significantly. Gram-positive bacteria (i.e. Micrococcus luteus and Staphylococcus aureus (with inhibition zones) contain teichoic acid in their cell walls, while, Gram-negative bacteria (i.e. Escherichia coli and Helicobacter pylori) lack this component [21]. In acidic environments, hydrogen ions (protons) can interact with functional groups on the surface of HEC-AM@N–CDs, often containing oxygen or nitrogen. This protonation can alter the energy levels involved in fluorescence, potential shift in the emitted color.

The negatively charged teichoic acid present in Gram-positive bacteria offer a new anionic sites compared with lipopolysaccharides in Gram-negative bacteria. This facilitates the electrostatic binding of cationic groups to the bacterial cell surface [20]. In contrast, the negatively charged lipopolysaccharides present in Gram-negative bacteria make it more difficult for cationic N–groups of HEC-AM@N–CDs to bind. The peptidoglycan layer in Gram-positive bacteria contains numerous hydrophobic regions, allowing for hydrophobic interactions with the HEC-AM@N–CDs structure. Additionally, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of peptidoglycan can form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. These interactions enable HEC-AM@N–CDs to rapidly target peptidoglycan in Gram-positive bacteria [20,44]. This is why the red color in the case of Gram-positive bacteria.

The results indicate that the CDs in HEC-AM@N–CDs were internalized into cells by entrapping endosomes, multivesicular bodies, and lysosomes via endocytosis [18,44]. The hydrophilic nature of the prepared CDs, with active functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxy and carboxylic acid, contributes to their efficient internalization into the cells [18].

Fig. S2 illustrates the surface morphology of the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels. Elemental analysis using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) revealed a composition of 54.15 % carbon (C), 4.11 % nitrogen (N), and 41.74 % oxygen (O).

The swelling ratio initially increases first rapidly before slowing down and reaching a maximum continuous swelling. The equilibrium swelling (sweq.%) of the HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs gels was measured at 4 h with a pH of 7.0 to be 60.97 and 63.29 %, respectively. HEC is a natural polymer. The hydrophilic nature of N–CDs in the HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogel contributes to the increased sweq.%. The presence of numerous highly charged groups in N–CDs leads to strong electrostatic interactions within the HEC-AM@N–CDs structure. The positively charged groups produced by nitrogen hetero-atoms in N–CDs enhance the electrostatic double-layer attraction to acrylate negative ions which generated from acrylamide hydrolysis. A pseudo-first-order kinetic proved to be more accurate in predicting for the swelling behavior of both HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels compared to the zero-order and the pseudo-second-order models. The K2 and coefficient of correlation (R2) values were higher for HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels, provides the best We2 value, suggesting a chemisorption mechanism [6].

For HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogels; n = 0.11 and 0.43; indicates Fickian diffusion [3,14]. The D value of HEC-AM was determined to be 0.036, which is likely due to its lower swelling capacity. The addition of N–CDs in HEC-AM@N–CDs resulted in a decrease in the D value to i.e. 0.024 [14]. As the HEC-AM@N–CDs swells, the HEC-AM@N–CDs polymer chains can become more entangled and packed. This can hinder the movement of solvent molecules, leading to a decrease in the D. The υ of HEC-AM@N–CDs is higher than that of HEC-AM indicating its greater swelling capacity [6,14].

Fig. 7 g and Table S2 illustrates the response of HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs to pH values ranging from 3 to 13. Both hydrogels exhibited the highest swelling ratio at pH 13. The presence of positively charged N–CDs in HEC-AM@N–CDs allowed for a significant change in volume over a wide pH range. At pH 3 and 7, the carboxylic group (–COOH) becomes protonated, leading to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. These bonds can limit anion-anion electrostatic repulsion, resulting in ion pair formation and a decrease in osmotic pressure within the hydrogel. Consequently, the network tends to shrink and collapse, leading to a reduction in sweq.% for both HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs. [14,45]. In alkaline medium (i.e. pH 9 and 13), the carboxylic group becomes ionised (–COO−). Additionally, the positive charge from N–CDs is maintained. This combination results in stronger hydrophilicity and higher electrostatic repulsion within the network, leading to an increased capacity for HEC-AM@N–CDs to absorb water compared to HEC-AM [14].

The thermo-responsiveness of HEC-AM and HEC-AM@N–CDs resulted in continuous increase in swelling ratio with varying reaction parameters when temperatures were changed from 30 to 60 °C (Fig. 7 h). Overall, the swelling of HEC-AM decreased as temperature increased. The introduced hydrophilic monomers (i.e. N–CDs) promotes the formation of H–bonds. The swelling of HEC-AM@N–CDs begins to rise from 30 to 40 °C. This might result from a rise in thermal mobility of polymer molecules inside the HEC-AM@N–CDs [14]. The swelling starts to go down between 50 and 60 °C for HEC-AM@N–CDs. This might be because the positively charged N–CDs will interact with the negatively charged –COO− groups, creating a chemical cross-linking which lead to HEC-AM@N–CDs shrink and, as a result, its swelling ratio to decrease [14,26].

The liquid microwave method was used to prepare N–CDs from SB by using an eco-friendly, fast and low cost simple technique. The prepared N–CDs were used to achieve the HEC-AM@N–CDs hydrogel. The HEC-AM@N–CDs' high swelling (63.29 %) likely result from N–CDs (low in HEC-AM). The presence of N–CDs promotes antibacterial activity of HEC-AM@N–CDs towards both Gram negative and positive bacterial compared to HEC-AM. The study found that HEC-AM@N–CDs interacted differently with Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria due to their distinct cell wall compositions. Gram-positive bacteria, with their negatively charged teichoic acid, facilitated stronger binding with the cationic groups on HEC-AM@N–CDs, leading to rapid targeting of the peptidoglycan layer. This resulted in red fluorescence emission. Conversely, Gram-negative bacteria with less negatively charged lipopolysaccharides showed weaker interaction and green fluorescence emission. Furthermore, the study suggests that the CDs within HEC-AM@N–CDs are internalized by Gram-positive bacteria through endocytosis. The DFT calculations and the molecular docking data proved the strong chelation between the bacteria and HEC-AM@N–CDs.

Hebat-Allah S. Tohamy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors appreciate the National Research Centre and the PRIMA International Project, between the Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT) and PRIMA, through the Joint Agreement project “Sustainable Antimicrobial Packaging based on a Healthy Intelligent Renewable Approach” with acronym “SAPHIRA”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.137389.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthetic strategies, photoluminescence mechanisms and promising applications of carbon dots: current state and future perspective
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. He, P. Xu, X. Zhang, W. Long, The synthetic strategies, photoluminescence mechanisms and promising applications of carbon dots: Current state and future perspective. Carbon, 186 (2022) 91-127.
